title: Polished Brand, Dysfunctional Reality
pros: Strong brand recognition. Well known name on a resume. Pay and benefits are generally in line with large corporations. This may be a suitable environment for those who prioritize brand name over clarity, fairness, and consistency. Others should proceed with caution.
cons: Day-to-day operations do not align with the companyâs external image. Leadership quality is uneven, and accountability at senior levels is inconsistent. Decisions are frequently made without transparency, with shifting direction and unclear ownership creating ongoing instability for teams. Open communication is limited. Raising concerns or asking for clarity can result in loss of visibility, exclusion from decision-making, or reassignment rather than constructive resolution. Performance management is subjective and inconsistently applied, making expectations unclear and long-term success unpredictable. While values, inclusion, and well-being are heavily emphasized in messaging, they are not consistently reflected in practice. Psychological safety is limited, particularly during periods of change or for those who do not fully align with leadership preferences.

title: Toxic culture filled with nepotism
pros: The Annual Bonus is the only highlight of working at Mars and the associates in all of the China offices are engaging and impressive.
cons: Prepare yourself to work with family favorites who have no clue on how to actually make a decision or let alone use a computer. You will be amazed to see how much money the company wastes on sponsoring below average talent who have no legal status, but have connections and murky ties to the business. There is no equity in terms of access to resources. Associates in West Africa are barely acknowledged for their work and existence, however leadership practically worships the Associates in Europe and India in comparison.

title: EXTREMELY good culture and work-life balance
body: EXTREMELY good culture and work-life balance
pros: I previously worked in finance, Mars is definitely a completely different vibe. People have very strong respect for each other and work-life balance, 9-5 style here. Very flat hierarchy, I can talk to senior directors who are 3/4 layers below the CEO any time I want. Dog-friendly, wfh-friendly, flexible working. Tons of great events for graduates, like proper Halloween gatherings, Christmas events, etc. (tho I suppose it's not specific to here, but they do put in strong effort)
cons: Some offices are quite far (e.g., Slough, Waltham, Castle Cary), but these offices tend to have very strong wfh (once a week, sometimes even once a month if its VERY far). Diversity could be better, though there's very strong active effort from management to recruit not just white people lol Top 80 percentile salary, so it's really decent, but don't expect IBD or Consulting-like compensations (obviously lol)

title: Hard to move up

cons: Feels impossible to move up from the factory unless your favored or know someone. They push career progression throughout orientation but is rarely seen past that. Leadership makes good workers feel used. Very high turnover, Starting pay rises faster than raises for current associates Very big disconnect between the factory and office/upper management. They push Safety and quality hard on associates, but bend the rules all the time to push production. Reactive as opposed to proactive in terms of fixing stuff which makes it very stressful on factory workers who have to work around or fix stuff constantly. Recent abrupt schedule change that split up associates, the selection process for who got what allegedly had guidelines, but was very obviously twisted for certain people. There is no work life balance depending on where you go, if your on straight nights you never have energy in work let alone outside of it.

title: Mars has an odd culture
pros: Free candy and ice cream if you want it. Most of the people who work there are nice.
cons: Manage by fear. Constant turnover. No clear expectations on how to perform your role. Each manager seemed to run their team different. Ex - Some managers didn't care if you wore jeans to work, Another would say no jean. Both teams are performing almost the same function in an office without direct customer contact but the expectation on how they dress is totally different. Employee reviews are based off of how well you performed against your peers, not how well you performed. Ex - eveyone met their goals but one person on the team exceed them by 10%. Why didn't you meet that same goal? You need to perform better. If you want me to meet a certain standard, then set it. Don't set the standard then complain that I didn't exceed it.

title: Stable Company with Great Benefits but Traditional Corporate Environment
body: Stable Company with Great Benefits but Traditional Corporate Environment
pros: Excellent long-term career stability - Many associates have been with the company for 10-15+ years, indicating strong retention and employee satisfaction Exceptional work-life balance - Working on England hours means finishing around 2pm, providing more personal time in the evenings Comprehensive compensation package: Short-term incentives Long-term incentives Annual bonuses Salary growth that rewards loyalty and tenure Engaging company culture with regular events: Super Bowl giveaways Halloween-themed celebrations Other social activities throughout the year Premium office environment: Brand new, modern facilities Abundant complimentary snacks and candies Well-designed workspace
cons: Traditional corporate structure with noticeable office politics Heavy reliance on external consultants (particularly Accenture) for technology implementation and support Bureaucratic processes that can slow down work: Need to submit tickets for many requests 2-3 day processing times for standard tasks Multiple permissions required for routine activities Limited technological autonomy for employees due to strict access controls and approval requirements

title: Mars is a great employer!
pros: Mars feels like a family (I know it sounds cliche but its true!). Every day, I come into work and can by myself and never be judged. Everyone is very accepting and down to earth. The pace of the work is very manageable, and my colleagues are always willing to help no matter how busy they are.
cons: Like all jobs, the hours can be long. We go through busy seasons but that is all a part of the field that I am in. Technically not really a con as the people I work with make the long days go by quicker.

title: Great place to work
pros: Alongside the great benefits at Mars, there is a strong culture of personal development. The ability to move around the business and learn new skills, is a great part of being with Mars. The people are great too, they make the difference. I've worked for Mars for nearly 28 years and it's helped me to achieve a lot in my life.
cons: The recruitment process can be tough, but I guess that's how they get the best people.

title: Evaluate my work at Mars
pros: The perfect implementation of the Five Principles and the concept of colleagues has greatly enhanced my job dedication and professionalism, giving me clear goals and directions in my work. Focusing on team building has enabled me to work in an atmosphere of mutual assistance and progress, greatly improving the efficiency and cohesion of the team.
cons: The company has always been dedicated to building its corporate culture, and everyone has a strong sense of cohesion. But you can consider organizing more outdoor expansion activities or team building games, allowing colleagues from different departments to deepen their understanding in a relaxed and enjoyable atmosphere, and further enhance team collaboration skills.

title: Horrible for career growth in Data & Analytics. HR (P&O) does nothing to help. Very political.
body: Horrible for career growth in Data & Analytics. HR (P&O) does nothing to help. Very political.
pros: Nicer than most large companies, more people-centric. Global roles can be quite interesting.
cons: Very political, especially at VP level. Nice VPs are rare. Many don't respond if you reach out, which is vastly different vs the lower-ranked employees, who tend to be more open. ZERO career growth in Data & Analytics. Not many roles open up, no automatic promotions or meaningful pay raises, you're just expected to continue in your current role or move "horizontally" without any growth in title or salary for years. HR doesn't care and in fact often hasn't even worked anywhere else besides Mars, so they don't realize that this isn't the "norm" and it's not motivational. Rather than looking for ways to promote development, they mostly just raise barriers. Talent leaves Data & Analytics and nothing is being done to address this. A shame, because there are a lot of good people here wasting away.

title: Impossible to get career growth in Data & Analytics, HR (P&O) has FAR TOO MUCH SAY on promotions
body: Impossible to get career growth in Data & Analytics, HR (P&O) has FAR TOO MUCH SAY on promotions
pros: More people-centric than most employers
cons: Extremely political at the VP level HR has way too much say on things like career growth - they don't provide anything positive on this, only putting up blockers. They don't understand the business AT ALL, like data & analytics -- they're clueless about talent (pay, career trajectories, etc) yet act all high & mighty and flex their "muscle" more often than they should. Why is HR doing nothing but blocking things rather than looking for ways to promote long-term career growth? It's amazing to see someone so clueless have so much impact. Other companies & industries don't have such blockers in HR like Mars does. A shame, lots of D&A talent leaving because HR is terrible.

pros: Good health benefits and can bring pets to the office
cons: The company treats better pets than their employees. The leadership team was micromanaging me. I had two managers and each one of them had different requirements/communication style. Even though I tried to meet their expectations, they would lie about me not doing my job. They made me read a book about dealing with life failure and report to one of the managers about my thoughts which obviously had to be in line with the book. I had to find 'role models' within my team and take example from them which was basically bunch of same old corporate bs. I had a deadlines to do all that, otherwise I'd be fired... At the same time I had a difficult personal situation I was going through and one of the managers forced on me to tell them the details. It was the most humiliating experience I've ever had in my professional career. I will never recommend this company to anyone as this was so traumatising.

title: I will never understand people who rate it 5
body: I will never understand people who rate it 5
pros: Brands, the business has a tremendous potential if itâs ever managed well.
cons: The way youâre treated in the company is going to depend a lot on who from senior leaders likes you and this proliferates a culture of âgood news onlyâ and hypocrisy. For a company that has mutuality written on the wall, Mars can treat you very cold. Processes are barely there, so your performance rating, salary increase and career progression depends on how well you smile and make connections, results are rarely a driver of career progression there. Systems are old and disjointed, the company understands it needs to change but the senior management is not enabling it, so constant transformations look more like corporate gaslighting than anything else. Surprisingly hierarchical. Donât believe over my tenure of 6 years a single HR person has spoken to me once.
location: Slough, Berkshire, South East England, England

title: Worst experience of my career
pros: Flexibility and work/life balance. Some nice people
cons: Feels like a cult. Those who are different, ask questions or don't agree with everything management say are made to feel they don't belong. I was actually told by my line manager not to challenge anything and just say 'yes' to whatever was asked - that way I would be perceived more positively by senior management. Unbelievably, she also informed the team that she had selected a particular new hire, not because he had the best experience or skills, but because it helped with the diversity stats. Sadly, he didn't last very long in the business. Lots of talk about diversity, but doesn't happen in practice. Opportunities for those who fit the mould (young, social, agreeable). Old boys club at senior management level.

title: Disgraceful
pros: Work for a widely recognized candy company
cons: This company falls surprisingly short of expectations. This is a globalist organization, and you know how the USA feels about globalism - no good. This company feels more like a public company than private, seemingly more run by the UK than the USA. Furthermore this is an elitist and feminist organization. They have extreme hiring preference towards those who went to only top colleges, especially colleges in Pennsylvania. There is also extreme focus on women being hired and promoted at fast pace on a yearly basis. Contractors are treated like the second-rate help. These are all forms of discrimination to those who do not fit their mold. The company itself is quite detached, there might be people at their desks but they are all inside their headphones in countless meetings. The place is very empty when it comes to actual intelligence and social connections. I would say the company has greatly lost its roots from its original founder.

title: Leaders make you feel small and insignificant, but benefits are great...
body: Leaders make you feel small and insignificant, but benefits are great...
pros: Pay is acceptable, benefits are wonderful. Free candy and dog food. Good prizes and food at company outings.
cons: Certain managers threaten to fire almost the entire team on a daily basis, and constantly tell employees they are not good enough. The next minute they will ask you to come in early or work overtime in the same position they just said you are no good at... The atmosphere is so negative here, and no one can be trusted. There is much favoritism and raises/promotions are based on that. They have people called Ombudsman who are supposedly neutral contacts to help resolve issues - they are far from neutral and also far from helpful. They back Mars supervisors or managers each and every time they are contacted, with little to no regard for regular employees or the situations they have been backed into.

title: Great Place to Work
pros: I really love the culture at Mars. The 5 Principles are actually something that's embedded in decision making and not just words on a website or wall. As an associate I feel cared for and taken care of by the company. Everything from good benefits to being able to work hybrid to senior leaders encouraging us to take care of ourselves and each other to the focus on improving inclusion and diversity at all levels.
cons: The only downside to Mars is that as a company, it can be quite risk adverse. This causes decisions to commit to something to take a long time and quick reactions when the risk level increases.

Opportunities are available for Associates to move laterally, vertically, cross-functions, cross-segments, and even cross-country! The opportunities are endless, and Mars' commitment to nurture Associates' learning, development, and overall wellbeing are ever present.

Mars people leader framework is keen on micromanaging while also lacking experience to actually lead people. There could be way more intellectual capacity leveraged from their workforce but the company is so far behind on current practices for developing their people. Main issues are management.

pros: Nothing as such is positive

Leadership team role changes in every 3 years thus each leader prepares strategy which are for short term benefit, ignoring the long term interest of the organisation. 3. Favouritism is at its peak. People with zero technical skills can reach higher position while some people with good technical struggle to get a promotion. 4. There is no data in the company across all functions for any kind of analysis 5. Forget about work life balance, you'll keep on working and there will be zero recognition for the good work you'll be do. 6. Manager do not have leadership skill, they keep on thinking about their benefit and promotion and don't focus on subordinate's development.

title: LaLaLand in midst of supply crisis | CEAB&T
body: LaLaLand in midst of supply crisis | CEAB&T
pros: - You work with leader products in the category: almost all brands are leaders in particular types with solid legacies. You represent them and make contribution to their growth and prosperity - There's a proper understanding of work-life balance on all levels: if you overwork occasionally, you take day-offs afterward - You are respected as an individual. When it comes to communication particularly, you would never have a manager shouting or humiliating you. - People around you are always ready to help when you ask for help. Some of them will even shock you with kindness and sincerity - Managers and leadership team are approachable. You can ask questions, advice, and discuss everyday business. - Decent office, hybrid work mode, flexibility, no micro-management
cons: - Problem sugar-coating: When there are challenges that need to be handled and resolved, the appropriate behavior is to bring exposure through demonstration of work that has no meaning in the particular situation - There's no track of future career development: even if they say that you should work hard, show off, and build credibility and image - all that has no meaning during the evaluations at assessment centers to the position openings. You have the same weight as someone from outside. You have it even less if your opponent is from Russia (regardless if it's from Mars Russia or not). - Strategy is a bubble: Strategies are made up around P&L numbers in Excel without a proper base in terms of market evaluation or supply chain feasibility.

title: Great Place to work
pros: It's a Family business with amazing brands behind the name. Although it is evolving in many ways, it still has this friendly charm of a family business. It's a company that is purpose driven, with huge focus on the environment and impact on communities. Decisions at Mars are made based on Five Principles; Responsibility, Mutual Benefit, Efficiency, Financial Freedom and Quality.
cons: The organisation is complex given it's evolution and the many acquired businesses, it can be overwhelming for a new joiner if they didn't have a solid onboarding plan. Difficult to navigate and understand who makes the decision. Working conditions in the factories are not consistently great. It varies from one country/ city to another and from one Segment to another. Decision making takes a long time, too many people need to be consulted and aligned. Changes that need to happen don't happen fast enough, and many times incomplete, that you hardly feel the desired change. Benefits, such as pension, are being reviewed and reduce over time and across different markets.

title: Great Company, Terrible Leadership
